summary: Jeff Atwood uses a reader's story about a disruptive team member obsessed with JavaScript to explore the broader problem of 'bad apples' on software teams. He argues that problem personnel who campaign against project decisions and work against teammates become serious liabilities, regardless of their technical intentions. Drawing on McConnell's research, Atwood emphasizes that team leaders who fail to address poor performers create the most consistent complaint among team members. The post provides warning signs for identifying bad apples and makes a clear case that managers must act decisively to remove disruptive personalities before they poison the entire project.
key_points: Problem team members who actively campaign against project decisions become liabilities regardless of their technical skills or intentions
Research shows the most intense complaint from team members is leaders unwilling to confront poor performers - a significant management blind spot
Warning signs include covering up ignorance, excessive privacy demands, territoriality, revisiting settled decisions, and not pitching in during crunch time
You can develop skill but you can't develop a positive attitude - attitude problems must be addressed through removal
Delayed action on problem personnel causes compounding damage through code, relationships, and team morale
money_quotes: At the point where you're actively campaigning against the project, and working against your teammates – you're a liability to the project.
You don't have to be friends with everyone on your team, although it certainly helps, but a level of basic personal and professional respect is mandatory for any team to function normally.
More than any other single aspect of team leadership, members are disturbed by leaders who are unwilling to deal directly and effectively with self-serving or noncontributing team members.
You can develop skill, but you can't develop a positive attitude.
If your team leader or manager isn't dealing with the bad apples on your project, she isn't doing her job.
Removing someone from a team is painful; it's not fun for anyone. But realizing you should have removed someone six months ago is far more painful.
They'll slowly spread poison throughout your project, in the form of code, relationships, and contacts.
key_insight: Team leaders who tolerate disruptive personalities aren't just failing that one relationship—they're signaling to every good developer that they'll be expected to succeed despite colleagues working against them.
